questions.question: Explain the three types of buses in a computer system with their functions and directionality. (5 marks)
keyPoints: Data Bus: Carries actual data between CPU, memory, and I/O. Bidirectional.
Address Bus: Carries memory/I/O addresses specified by the CPU. Unidirectional (CPU to memory/I/O). Width determines addressable memory (n lines = 2^n locations).
Control Bus: Carries timing and control signals (Read/Write, MREQ, IOREQ, interrupt lines, clock).

questions.question: Compare single bus and multiple bus organizations. Explain bus arbitration methods (centralized and distributed). (10 marks)
keyPoints: Single bus: All components share one bus. Simple, low cost, easy to add devices. Disadvantage: only one transfer at a time, bus becomes a bottleneck.
Multiple bus: Separate buses for different purposes (processor bus, memory bus, I/O bus). Higher performance through parallel transfers. More complex and costly.
Hierarchical bus: Modern PCs use processor bus (fastest), system/memory bus, I/O bus (PCIe), peripheral bus (USB, SATA) connected by bridges.
Centralized arbitration: A single bus controller receives bus requests and grants access. Examples: daisy-chain priority, polling.
Distributed arbitration: Each device has its own arbitration logic. Devices compete using a priority scheme. No single point of failure.
Bus cycle consists of address phase, data phase, and acknowledgment.
